facts.title: Foundation of the Duomo
facts.body: Construction of the Duomo di Milano began in 1386 under Archbishop Antonio da Saluzzo and continued for nearly six centuries, a span that marked Milan's transition from medieval commune to modern metropolis.

facts.title: Candoglia Marble
facts.body: Quarried at Lake Maggiore and floated in by canal, the distinctive pink-veined Candoglia marble gives the cathedral its characteristic blush tone. This choice reflects Milan's connection to northern Alpine trade routes.

facts.title: Gothic Spires
facts.body: The first gothic spires rise during the medieval period. The full set of 135 spires won't be completed until the 19th century, each topped with a statue or finial.

facts.title: The Pink Marble of Candoglia
facts.body: The Duomo is built almost entirely from Candoglia marble, a pink-grey stone quarried 100 km north of Milan. Gian Galeazzo Visconti granted the Cathedral's building authority (Fabbrica) perpetual rights to the quarry in 1387 — a grant still in force today.

facts.title: La Madonnina
facts.body: The 4.16-metre gilded copper statue of the Virgin Mary, known as La Madonnina, was placed atop the tallest spire in 1774 at a height of 108.5 metres. A Milanese custom held that no building should rise above her until 1958, when the first skyscrapers of the Porta Nuova area broke that informal rule.

source.label: Comune di Milano — Duomo heritage page

facts.title: Piazza Duomo during WWII
facts.body: In August 1943 Allied bombing severely damaged buildings around Piazza Duomo. The cathedral itself was struck but its main structure survived, and the square became a symbolic stage for Milan's 1945 liberation.

facts.title: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II
facts.body: Designed by Giuseppe Mengoni and completed between 1865 and 1877, the Galleria is one of the world's oldest shopping arcades and forms the northern edge of Piazza del Duomo.

facts.title: The Mosaic Floor and the Bull of Turin
facts.body: The Galleria's floor is decorated with the coats of arms of Milan, Turin, Florence, and Rome in mosaic. The bull emblem of Turin, located at the centre of the octagonal crossing, is worn down from the tradition of spinning on one's heel for good luck — a custom that continues to this day.

source.label: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II — Comune di Milano

facts.title: The Galleria After the 1943 Bombing
facts.body: The 1943 Allied air raids destroyed the Galleria's glass vaulting and badly damaged its interior. Reconstruction of the iron-and-glass roof took until 1948, and the arcade reopened as a symbol of Milanese resilience and the city's economic recovery.

facts.title: Palazzo Reale — From Commune to Court
facts.body: Originally the seat of Milan's medieval communal government in the 12th century, Palazzo Reale was transformed into the ducal court of the Visconti and later the Sforza. It hosted Leonardo da Vinci during his time in Milan (1482–1499), when he was employed by Ludovico Sforza.

facts.title: Palazzo Reale After WWII
facts.body: Like much of central Milan, Palazzo Reale was heavily damaged in the 1943 bombing raids. Its restoration took decades; the Neoclassical interiors were painstakingly rebuilt and the building reopened progressively from the 1950s onward as a cultural and exhibition venue rather than a seat of power.

facts.title: Palazzo Reale Today — Italy's Exhibition Hub
facts.body: Today Palazzo Reale is one of Italy's most visited temporary exhibition venues, attracting over 600,000 visitors a year. It hosts major international art shows and houses a permanent collection of Milanese decorative arts. Entry to the piazza-facing courtyard is free.
